title: SEGMENT 6 — Pembrolizumab and nivolumab: the PD-1 dynasty

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Pembrolizumab and nivolumab: the PD-1 dynasty
If ipilimumab was the first brake-cutter, pembrolizumab and nivolumab are the brake-cutters that became blockbusters.
They are, between them, the two best-selling oncology drugs in the world, and pembrolizumab — Merck's Keytruda — is now the single best-selling drug of any kind in the world.
In 2024 it generated something north of twenty-nine billion dollars in revenue.
That number is not a typo.
One drug.
Twenty-nine billion dollars.
Both drugs are anti-PD-1 antibodies.
They are fully or nearly fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibodies that bind PD-1 on T cells and block it from interacting with its ligands PD-L1 and PD-L2.
The IgG4 choice matters — IgG4 has weak effector function, so it doesn't kill the T cells it binds; it just sits on PD-1 and blocks the engagement.
The drugs are, in essence, blindfolds — they cover the eyes of the T cell so it can't see the white flag the tumor is waving.
Pembrolizumab and nivolumab were developed in parallel, by Merck and Bristol-Myers Squibb respectively.
They have very similar mechanisms, similar efficacy, similar toxicity profiles.
They were both approved in late 2014 for metastatic melanoma.
Then, in 2015, both were approved for non-small-cell lung cancer — and that is the approval that made them giants.
Lung cancer is the largest oncology market in the world.

About a quarter of patients with non-small-cell lung cancer respond to anti-PD-1 therapy, and a subset of those responders — particularly those with high PD-L1 expression on their tumors — get durable remissions.
For decades, advanced lung cancer had a five-year survival rate of less than five percent.
With pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy as front-line therapy, that number is now closer to twenty-five percent in PD-L1-positive patients.
We have, in less than ten years, quintupled five-year survival for one of the deadliest common cancers.

The clinical pattern with anti-PD-1 monotherapy is similar to ipilimumab but generally better: higher response rates — thirty to forty percent in melanoma, twenty to twenty-five percent in lung — fewer severe toxicities, and durable responses in those who respond.
The tail of the survival curve goes flat just as it did with Yervoy, but a larger fraction of patients end up on the flat part.
The toxicity profile is also more forgiving.
PD-1 inhibitors cause immune-related adverse events too — they have to, mechanistically, because PD-1 is also a brake on autoimmunity in normal tissues.
But the rate of severe toxicity is lower than with ipilimumab.
About ten to fifteen percent of patients get severe irAEs on PD-1 monotherapy, versus twenty-plus percent on ipilimumab.
The thyroid, the lungs, the colon, and the skin remain the most commonly affected organs.
Pembrolizumab has, over the past decade, accumulated an absolutely staggering set of approvals.

As of 2025, the FDA-approved indications for Keytruda include melanoma, non-small-cell lung cancer, small-cell lung cancer, head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, classical Hodgkin lymphoma, primary mediastinal B-cell lymphoma, urothelial carcinoma, microsatellite-instability-high or mismatch-repair-deficient solid tumors of any origin, gastric cancer, esophageal cancer, cervical cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, Merkel cell carcinoma, renal cell carcinoma, endometrial carcinoma, tumor-
mutational-burden-high solid tumors, cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, triple-negative breast cancer, biliary tract cancer — and the list keeps growing.
There is, at this point, a serious case that pembrolizumab will eventually be tested in essentially every solid tumor on Earth.
Nivolumab — Opdivo — has a slightly smaller but still vast list.
Both drugs have, between them, dozens of approved combination regimens with chemotherapy, with anti-angiogenic drugs, with other immunotherapies, with targeted therapies.
The PD-1 inhibitors are not just drugs.
They are platforms — building blocks that get combined with almost everything else.
And both drugs have transformed the commercial landscape of oncology.
Keytruda revenue alone is greater than the entire revenue of most large pharmaceutical companies.
Merck's whole modern business is, essentially, a Keytruda business with some other products attached.
When the Keytruda patent expires in 2028 — assuming current extensions hold — there will be a cliff.
Merck has spent much of the last five years frantically acquiring other companies and trying to develop next-generation immunotherapies to soften the fall.
The whole industry is watching.
The clinical lesson, for our purposes, is this.
Anti-PD-1 antibodies have become the most widely used cancer drugs in history.
They are the workhorses.
They are the foundation onto which combination therapies are layered.
Wherever there is a tumor that the immune system has tried to attack — wherever there are T cells in the tumor that have been turned off by PD-L1 — anti-PD-1 antibodies have a chance of working.
That includes a startling range of cancers.
The principle is exactly what Honjo described.
Block PD-1, the T cells already in the tumor stop accepting the stand-down signal, and they kill.
Same idea as Yervoy, different brake, broader applicability, gentler toxicity.
It is the second move in the chess game, and it turned out to be a better move than the first.
